title: Top Five Rock Movies, Plus Elisabeth Moss and Alex Ross Perry on ‘Her Smell’ | Interview
format: Sean and Lindsay Zoladz work through what makes a rock movie work, biopic vs. invented world, before Sean sits down with Elisabeth Moss and Alex Ross Perry on their third film together, 'Her Smell.'

segments.heading: What Makes a Rock Movie Work

films.title: Her Smell
films.note: Elisabeth Moss and Alex Ross Perry's film about Becky Something, a rock star in the Courtney Love mold who isn't Courtney Love. The frame for the whole conversation: fictionalized-but-inspired-by vs. the Bohemian Rhapsody-style biopic.

films.title: Teen Spirit
films.note: Max Minghella's pop-star story, Elle Fanning in the lead, opening the same week.

films.title: Bohemian Rhapsody
films.note: The biopic Sean and Lindsay measure everything else against: a huge hit despite being, in their view, dull and Oscar-shaped.

films.title: Vox Lux
films.note: Brady Corbet's Natalie Portman pop-stardom drama. Lindsay isn't a fan: she thinks Sia's songs for the film sound like Sia songs, not like 2001 pop.

films.title: Velvet Goldmine
films.note: Todd Haynes' glam-rock film, which Lindsay ranks well above Vox Lux: Christian Bale, Jonathan Rhys Meyers, and Ewan McGregor as an Iggy Pop type. Less about the David Bowie figure than about fandom itself.

films.title: Almost Famous
films.note: Cited as the model for centering the fan rather than the star.

films.title: Inside Llewyn Davis
films.note: Not strictly rock and roll, but Sean likes it for the same reason: it's about the slow, failure-strewn construction of a musician, not just the glory.

films.title: Purple Rain
films.note: Prince playing a version of himself under another name: the space between the movie's world and the real one it's clearly referencing.

films.title: That Thing You Do!
films.note: Sean's pick for the one-hit-wonder rise-and-fall story; he credits the fact that the songs are genuinely good, not just plausible.

films.title: Phantom of the Paradise
films.note: Brian De Palma's rock-opera horror-comedy with songs by Paul Williams. Lindsay's been thinking about it alongside Vox Lux and Her Smell for how early it cast the music business as literally demonic.

films.title: The Rocky Horror Picture Show
films.note: Comes up as the more famous cousin to Phantom of the Paradise, which actually predates it.

films.title: This Is Spinal Tap
films.note: The historic example of straight satire still holding up.

films.title: Popstar: Never Stop Never Stopping
films.note: The Lonely Island's pop-star spoof, a box-office failure that's built a strong cult afterlife.

films.title: Quadrophenia
films.note: One of the self-serious 'music of' movies Lindsay says hasn't aged well, alongside Tommy and The Wall.

films.title: The Who: The Kids Are Alright
films.note: Lindsay's case for why she doesn't love most rock biopics: this Who documentary already has the real performance footage a biopic can only approximate.

films.title: Walk the Line
films.note: Lindsay's favorite biopic of the bunch. She credits the performances for elevating it.

films.title: 24 Hour Party People
films.note: Sean's favorite side-door biopic: about a record executive, so it gets close to the artists without being about them.

films.title: Control
films.note: The Joy Division film. Sean calls it a beautiful, deeply dark movie, though he's not sure it's the movie he wants about Joy Division specifically.

films.title: Rocketman
films.note: The Elton John movie, still a month from release, raised as the next big communal-theater music event after Bohemian Rhapsody.

films.title: Climax
films.note: Gaspar Noé's dance-music freakout, another movie Sean and Lindsay talk about for how it lands as a physical, in-theater experience rather than for the story.

films.title: Rock 'n' Roll High School
films.note: The Ramones movie. Lindsay loves it for capturing the band's spirit of youthful rebellion without taking itself seriously at all.

films.title: Wild Rose
films.note: Sean's advance rec from CinemaCon: Jessie Buckley as a Scottish woman chasing a country career in Nashville.

films.title: Ladies and Gentlemen, the Fabulous Stains
films.note: Lindsay's closing pick and the segue into 'Her Smell': teenage Diane Lane and Laura Dern front a punk band. A cult movie she says helped seed the riot grrrl scene. Kathleen Hanna and Tobi Vail reportedly watched it together as teenagers.

segments.heading: In Conversation
segments.guest: Elisabeth Moss and Alex Ross Perry on

films.title: Her Smell
films.note: Their third film together, and the longest shoot of the three: four weeks, each of the five acts rehearsed for a day and shot chronologically within itself, though the acts weren't shot in story order. Perry says the idea started in 2015 as a two-word text: 'rock star mother addict.' Moss studied guitar for five months to look like she could play, working from a script she calls note-perfect, with no improv, even in Becky's ranting.

films.title: Queen of Earth
films.note: Their prior collaboration, the one where the 'Her Smell' idea first occurred to Perry. He floats it, half-joking, as the ideal double feature partner.

films.title: There Will Be Blood
films.note: Perry's dream-double-feature pick: Becky Something as 'the bride of Plainview.'

films.title: Steve Jobs
films.note: Perry calls it a direct influence on 'Her Smell,' both structured as discrete, theatrical acts: three for Danny Boyle's film, five for theirs.

films.title: Ladies and Gentlemen, the Fabulous Stains
films.note: Perry names it again as a visual and stylistic touchstone for the film, unprompted. It's the same pick Lindsay Zoladz closed the first segment with.

The Who: The Kids Are Alright.title: The Who: The Kids Are Alright

Ladies and Gentlemen, the Fabulous Stain.title: Ladies and Gentlemen, the Fabulous Stains
